title: Where the Iran Conflict Goes From Here
author: Dana Sherne
text:

Where the Iran Conflict Goes From Here

As the United States and Iran hit the escalate button once again, what will it take for both sides to pull back? One advisor the White House could have turned to was Nate Swanson, a career State Department official who served most recently as director for Iran at the National Security Council. But Swanson was forced out of his role by the Trump administration last year. Outside of government, he accurately predicted how Iran would respond to U.S. strikes and how it might overplay its hand after signing the memorandum of understanding.
Where does Swanson see the conflict headed next? What options does the White House have available to it?

Host and Guests

Nate Swanson

Director, Atlantic Council’s Iran Strategy Project

Nate Swanson is a resident senior fellow and director of the Iran Strategy Project at the Atlantic Council’s Scowcroft Middle East Security Initiative. He previously served in the State Department. He most recently served as director for Iran at the National Security Council, where he was on the Trump administration’s Iran negotiating team in the spring and summer of 2025.

Nate Swanson is a resident senior fellow and director of the Iran Strategy Project at the Atlantic Council’s Scowcroft Middle East Security Initiative. He previously served in the State Department. He most recently served as director for Iran at the National Security Council, where he was on the Trump administration’s Iran negotiating team in the spring and summer of 2025.

Ravi Agrawal

Editor in chief, Foreign Policy

Ravi Agrawal is the editor in chief of Foreign Policy and host of FP Live. Before joining FP in 2018, Agrawal worked at CNN for more than a decade, including as New Delhi bureau chief and correspondent. He is the author of India Connected: How the Smartphone Is Transforming the World’s Largest Democracy.

On-Demand from FP Live

Is Trans-Atlanticism Dead? A Debate.

July 14, 2026

U.S. President Donald Trump’s second term has forced a reckoning: Can the trans-Atlantic relationship be salvaged, or is the damage permanent? Nathalie Tocci argues it cannot be rebuilt as it was: the liberal order that gave trans-Atlanticism its context is gone, and a fundamental breach of trust has occurred. Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer sees it differently: Trans-Atlanticism was never only based on values and Europe’s surge in defense spending signals its emergence as a geopolitically mature partner. They joined FP’s Ravi Agrawal for a debate.

Ukraine’s New Momentum?

July 9, 2026

When NATO leaders convened this week in Ankara, Turkey, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky again pressed them to cement their support for Kyiv. But this time, he could argue that Ukraine’s long-range drones have brought the war home to Russians, a strain that even President Vladimir Putin has recently acknowledged. Can battlefield momentum translate into movement in the peace talks? How much does Kyiv still need Washington? Dmytro Kuleba, Ukraine’s foreign minister from 2020 to 2024, joined FP’s Ravi Agrawal to assess where the war goes from here.

Is Cuba Next?

June 30, 2026

After the United States ousted Venezuela’s Nicolás Maduro in January and took out Iran’s senior leadership in February, U.S. President Donald Trump set his sights on Cuba, claiming that he would have the “honor of taking” the island. Now, after five months of a U.S. oil blockade, Havana is restructuring its economy and even publicly welcoming diplomacy with Washington. So, is a new form of regime change underway in Cuba, or will the Trump administration seek to replicate the model of Venezuela or Iran? Michael Bustamante, a leading historian of Cuban-American relations, joined FP Live to assess the White House’s options.

What SpaceX Means for the World

June 25, 2026

SpaceX’s successful listing on Nasdaq (briefly) turned CEO Elon Musk into the world’s first trillionaire—and the most powerful person deciding the planet’s role in space. What does our new era of space capitalism mean for the role of states? And specifically in Musk’s case, does one man have too much power? Historian Quinn Slobodian, co-author of the new book Muskism, argues that Musk has designs on controlling discourse on Earth—powered by technology in space. He joined FP Live to discuss how countries and companies can navigate Musk’s dominance.
